summary: Stancil argues that the database world has swung from boxed software to cloud bundles to a fragmented “separate storage/compute/engine” stack that’s powerful but complicated, costly, and hard to use—creating demand for a bundled, simplified “Postgres in a box” again. He extends the idea to unstructured data and LLMs, suggesting that once we can query text and files like tables, content platforms become part of the same database story. The post ends by contrasting flashy AI insights with the enduring value of straightforward analytics that simply tell people what’s happening.
money_quotes: So that’s what databases did. They split themselves in half; they “separated storage from compute,” as the catchphrase goes.
You can’t put your data in one place, and bring your favorite calculator to it.
Your one database becomes a modern data stack of databases, in the worst way: A knot of intertwined and opaque costs.
Data is still downstream of emotions and biases; a quantitative survey of 10,000 people is no less corruptible than a qualitative interview panel of 10 people.
But that really is all we want.
key_insight: The march toward modular, interoperable data stacks is real, but the resulting complexity and cost make the market ripe for a re-bundled, simpler “database-in-a-box” that even extends to unstructured data via LLMs.
